basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: An-Nās — “Mankind” — is the one hundred and fourteenth and final sūra of the Quran. Together with the preceding sūra, al-Falaq, it forms the Muʿawwidhatān, “the two protectresses”: the two sūras of refuge. Both open with the same command — Say: I seek refuge… But where al-Falaq protects from outward harms, an-Nās guards the most intimate place: the heart. It asks for refuge from the whisperer, the one who breathes doubt within man.

versets.trad: Say: “I seek refuge with the Lord of mankind,
versets.commentaire: Three divine Names are about to be pronounced, in an ascent. The first is Rabb, the Lord — the One who nourishes, raises, makes grow; the One from whom existence is held at every instant. To seek refuge is first to recognise oneself as wholly dependent — the very opposite of the illusion of self-sufficiency.

mots.gram: preposition bi- + noun in the genitive

versets.commentaire: The second Name: Malik, the King — the One who governs, from whom all true authority derives. After the One who brings into being, the One who rules: the world is not abandoned to chance, it is held.

The third Name: Ilāh, the God — the sole One towards whom worship goes. The ascent is complete: He brings us into being (Rabb), He governs us (Malik), He alone is worthy of worship (Ilāh). Three ways of being bound to Him — and it is in forgetting them that man arrogates to himself a self-sufficiency that belongs to God alone.

versets.trad: from the harm of the furtive whisperer,
versets.commentaire: al-waswās al-khannās: the furtive whisperer. Waswasa is the murmur — a suggestion uttered low, and repeated. Khannās designates the one who withdraws, who draws back and then returns. The tradition explains it thus: the whisperer of doubt slips away as soon as the heart remembers God, and returns as soon as the heart forgets Him. The remedy is therefore inscribed in the very gesture of the sūra.

versets.trad: who whispers doubt into the breasts of mankind —
versets.commentaire: He whispers into the breasts of mankind — ṣudūr, the inner chamber of the heart. Temptation is not a cry from outside: it is intimate, it speaks from within, in a low voice. The commentators ask: is it the devil who murmurs, or the soul itself? The tradition holds both — the suggestion comes, and the soul that commands to evil (al-nafs al-ammāra) consents to it. The struggle is played out in that narrow space between the suggestion received and the consent given.

versets.trad: whether of jinn or of mankind.”
versets.commentaire: The whisperer is of two orders: the jinn — the unseen — and mankind. Evil counsel does not come only from a hidden world: it reaches us also through other human beings. And the whole Quran closes here on the word an-nās, “mankind” — the same word that has scanned the entire sūra, repeated like a refrain. The Book ends upon man: the one it has, from beginning to end, sought to awaken.
